content:
# Numbers Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 167-169 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_016_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0124-0125

---

**1** And Korah son of Izhar, son of Kohath, son of Levi, took men -- along with Dathan and Abiram, sons of Eliab, and On son of Peleth, sons of Reuben --

**2** and they rose up before Moses, together with two hundred and fifty men from the Sons of Israel, leaders of the community, representatives chosen by the assembly, men of renown.

**3** And they gathered against Moses and against Aaron and said to them, "You have gone too far! The entire community is holy, every one of them, and YHWH is among them. So why do you elevate yourselves above the assembly of YHWH?"

**4** And when Moses heard this, he fell on his face.

**5** And he spoke to Korah and to all his followers, saying, "In the morning YHWH will make known who belongs to him and who is holy, and he will draw that one near to himself. The one whom he chooses, he will draw near to himself.

**6** Do this: Take for yourselves fire-pans -- Korah and all his followers --

**7** and put fire in them and place incense on them before YHWH tomorrow. And the man whom YHWH chooses -- he is the holy one. You have gone too far, sons of Levi!"

**8** And Moses said to Korah, "Listen now, sons of Levi!

**9** Is it too small a thing for you that the God of Israel has set you apart from the community of Israel, bringing you near to himself to perform the service of the tabernacle of YHWH and to stand before the community to minister to them?

**10** He has brought you near, and all your brothers the sons of Levi with you -- and now you seek the priesthood as well?

**11** Therefore it is against YHWH that you and all your followers have gathered together. And Aaron -- what is he that you should grumble against him?"

**12** And Moses sent word to summon Dathan and Abiram, sons of Eliab. But they said, "We will not come up!

**13** Is it too small a thing that you brought us up out of a land flowing with milk and honey to kill us in the wilderness, that you must also lord it over us, making yourself a ruler?

**14** Moreover, you have not brought us into a land flowing with milk and honey, nor given us an inheritance of fields and vineyards. Would you gouge out the eyes of these men? We will not come up!"

**15** And Moses became very angry and said to YHWH, "Do not pay any regard to their offering. I have not taken so much as a single donkey from them, and I have not wronged a single one of them."

**16** And Moses said to Korah, "You and all your followers -- present yourselves before YHWH, you and they and Aaron, tomorrow.

**17** And let each man take his fire-pan and place incense on it, and each man shall bring his fire-pan before YHWH -- two hundred and fifty fire-pans -- and you and Aaron, each with his fire-pan."

**18** And each man took his fire-pan and put fire in them and placed incense on them, and they stood at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, along with Moses and Aaron.

**19** And Korah assembled the entire community against them at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting. And the glory of YHWH appeared to the entire community.

**20** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

**21** "Separate yourselves from this community, and I will consume them in an instant."

**22** And they fell on their faces and said, "O God -- God of the spirits of all flesh -- shall one man sin and you pour out your wrath on the entire community?"

**23** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**24** "Speak to the community, saying, 'Move away from all around the dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.'"

**25** And Moses rose and went to Dathan and Abiram, and the elders of Israel followed him.

**26** And he spoke to the community, saying, "Turn away, please, from the tents of these wicked men, and do not touch anything that belongs to them, lest you be swept away in all their sins."

**27** And they moved away from the dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram on every side. And Dathan and Abiram came out and stood at the entrance of their tents, with their wives, their sons, and their small children.

**28** And Moses said, "By this you shall know that YHWH has sent me to do all these deeds, and that none of this comes from my own heart:

**29** If these men die the common death of all humanity, and the fate of all humanity is visited upon them, then YHWH has not sent me.

**30** But if YHWH creates something utterly new, and the ground opens its mouth and swallows them and everything that belongs to them, and they go down alive into Sheol -- then you will know that these men have treated YHWH with contempt."

**31** And the moment he finished speaking all these words, the ground beneath them split apart.

**32** And the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them and their households and every person who belonged to Korah and all their possessions.

**33** And they and everything that belonged to them went down alive into Sheol, and the earth closed over them, and they perished from the midst of the assembly.

**34** And all Israel who were around them fled at the sound of their cries, for they said, "The earth might swallow us too!"

**35** And fire came out from YHWH and consumed the two hundred and fifty men who were offering the incense.

**36** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**37** "Tell Eleazar son of Aaron the priest to remove the fire-pans from the blaze, and scatter the fire far away, for they have become holy --

**38** the fire-pans of these men who sinned at the cost of their own lives. Let them be hammered into sheets as a covering for the altar, for they were brought before YHWH and so have become holy. And they shall serve as a sign to the Sons of Israel."

**39** And Eleazar the priest took the bronze fire-pans that those who had been burned had brought, and they were hammered out as a covering for the altar --

**40** a reminder to the Sons of Israel that no outsider who is not of the seed of Aaron should approach to burn incense before YHWH, so that he does not become like Korah and his followers -- just as YHWH had spoken to him through Moses.

**41** And the very next day the entire community of the Sons of Israel grumbled against Moses and against Aaron, saying, "You have killed the people of YHWH!"

**42** And when the community assembled against Moses and against Aaron, they turned toward the Tent of Meeting, and there the cloud had covered it, and the glory of YHWH appeared.

**43** And Moses and Aaron came to the front of the Tent of Meeting.

**44** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**45** "Get away from this community, and I will consume them in an instant." And they fell on their faces.

**46** And Moses said to Aaron, "Take the fire-pan and put fire from the altar in it, and place incense on it, and carry it quickly to the community and make atonement for them -- for the wrath has gone out from YHWH; the plague has begun!"

**47** And Aaron took it, just as Moses had spoken, and ran into the midst of the assembly -- and indeed, the plague had already begun among the people. And he placed the incense on it and made atonement for the people.

**48** And he stood between the dead and the living, and the plague was stopped.

**49** And those who died from the plague were fourteen thousand seven hundred, besides those who died on account of Korah.

**50** And Aaron returned to Moses at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, and the plague had been stopped.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 -- "took men":** The Hebrew verb *wayyiqqach* ("and he took") appears without a direct object, creating a jarring, abrupt opening. The Leningrad Codex preserves this syntactical rupture. The LXX (Vaticanus) smooths this by supplying a clear verb of speech/action. I have retained the sense of conspiratorial gathering by rendering "took men," making the implicit object explicit as the narrative demands for English comprehension while preserving the verb's aggressive connotation of seizing initiative.

**Verse 3 -- "You have gone too far!" (rav lakhem):** Literally "much/enough for you." This is a charged idiom of confrontation -- essentially "you've overstepped." Moses throws the identical phrase back at the Levites in verse 7 (*rav lakhem bene Levi*), creating a devastating rhetorical mirror. I have rendered both with "You have gone too far" to preserve this deliberate echo.

**Verse 13 -- "a land flowing with milk and honey":** Dathan and Abiram apply this covenantal phrase -- normally reserved for the Promised Land -- to *Egypt*, weaponizing sacred language in a breathtaking act of rhetorical inversion. No translation note can fully capture this shock; the reader must feel it in context.

**Verse 14 -- "gouge out the eyes":** The Hebrew *ha'ene ha'anashim hahem tenaqer* is a vivid idiom meaning "to deceive" or "to pull the wool over their eyes," but with far more violence -- literally proposing the gouging out of eyes. I have retained the literal image because the dynamic force of the metaphor is itself the meaning: Dathan and Abiram accuse Moses of blinding people to his tyranny.

**Verse 15 -- "not taken so much as a single donkey":** Moses' defense echoes the pattern later used by Samuel (1 Samuel 12:3). The donkey is not incidental -- it was the most basic unit of personal property. Moses claims total financial integrity. The LXX reads *epithumema* ("desirable thing"), slightly generalizing.

**Verse 22 -- "God of the spirits of all flesh":** The Hebrew *El elohe haruchot lekhol basar* is a remarkable theological title found only here and in Numbers 27:16. It positions YHWH as sovereign over the inner life-breath of every living being -- the God who knows that not every member of a community shares its leaders' guilt.

**Verse 30 -- "creates something utterly new" (beri'ah yivra YHWH):** The Hebrew uses the root *bara'* (to create) in a cognate accusative construction -- literally "if YHWH creates a creation." This is the infinitive absolute doubling convention: the word intensifies itself. YHWH is not simply acting; he is performing an act of *origination*, something unprecedented in the order of nature. I render this as "creates something utterly new" to carry the force of the doubled root.

**Verse 34 -- "at the sound of their cries":** The Hebrew *leqolam* -- "at their voice/sound." The screams of those descending alive into Sheol send all Israel fleeing. The narrative's acoustic horror is as central as its visual spectacle.

**Verse 38 -- "sinned at the cost of their own lives":** The Hebrew *hachatta'im ha'elleh benafshotam* carries the double meaning that their sin *was* their death -- the rebellion and its consequence are inseparable. The LXX renders with *hamartoloi houtoi en tais psuchais auton*, preserving the same ambiguity.

**Verse 41 -- "the very next day":** The Hebrew *mimmocharath* introduces one of the most astonishing turns in the narrative. The community has just witnessed the earth swallow rebels and fire consume two hundred and fifty men, yet *the very next morning* they accuse Moses and Aaron of murder. This is not mere stubbornness -- it is the deep human resistance to divine authority channeled through human mediators.

**Verse 48 -- "stood between the dead and the living":** One of the most iconic images in all of Torah. Aaron, the high priest, positions himself physically in the gap where death is advancing through the camp, and the plague halts at his person. The Hebrew is stark: *wayyaamod ben hammetim uven hachayim.* No elaboration is needed; the image carries its own weight.

-- PNEUMA
